column7: As the world’s largest palatial complex, it served as the imperial heart for 24 emperors of the Ming and Qing dynasties. Today, it preserves 980 historic buildings across a sprawling 72-hectare site.
column8: Hall of Supreme Harmony, Imperial Garden, treasure galleries
column9: Enter from south (Tiananmen), exit north. Rent audio guide at entrance. Bring passport for ticket pickup.
column10: Closed every Monday. Peak crowds Oct 1-7. No re-entry after exit.

column7: Located 70km north of the city, this section offers a more tranquil alternative to the bustling Badaling. It features breathtaking mountain ridges and a convenient cable car for easy access to its majestic watchtowers.
column8: Watchtowers 14-23 offer best views. Toboggan ride down.
column9: Take the cable car up, walk along the wall, toboggan down. Bring water and sunscreen. Wear hiking shoes.
column10: 90-min drive from city center. Plan a full half-day. Avoid weekends.

column7: A masterpiece of Ming dynasty craftsmanship where emperors once held grand rituals to pray for bountiful harvests. It is now set within a sprawling park where you can witness the charming daily life of local Beijingers.
column8: Hall of Prayer for Good Harvests, Echo Wall, park morning exercises
column9: Visit early morning (before 9am) to see locals doing tai chi and dancing. The park is free; temple complex requires ticket.
column10: Audio guide available in 40+ languages. South entrance closest to metro.

column7: One of Beijing's most iconic ancient hutongs. This lively pedestrian street is packed with boutique crafts, trendy cafes, and local snacks, while the quiet side alleys nearby offer a glimpse into the city's traditional soul.
column8: Side alleys (away from main street) show authentic hutong life. Local craft beer bars.
column9: Skip the main street crowds — duck into side alleys for the real experience. Great for photography. Easy to combine with Drum Tower.
column10: Very crowded on weekends and holidays. Main street is touristy; side alleys are authentic.
column11: Local Life, Local Snacks, Citywalk, Foodie, Instagram

column7: An award-winning pioneer of modern Peking duck. Renowned for its signature "super-lean" crispy skin and artistic culinary presentation, it remains a must-visit for a sophisticated dining experience.
column8: Signature lean-style Peking duck. Contemporary Chinese cuisine presentation.
column9: Reserve 1-2 days ahead. English menu with photos available. Dongcheng branch is most foreigner-friendly.
column10: Expect 300-500 CNY per person. Wait times can exceed 1 hour without reservation.

column7: The most famous and best-preserved section of the wall. It’s easy to hike and offers that classic "Great Wall" view you see in photos. "if you don't see the Great Wall, you are not a hero", it has become the first choice for global tourists to appreciate the majesty of the Great Wall. It’s easy to hike and offers that classic "Great Wall" view you see in photos. "if you don't see the Great Wall, you are not a hero", it has become the first choice for global tourists to appreciate the majesty of the Great Wall.
column8: North 8th Watchtower (Hao Han Po), "Hero's Stele," best-preserved ramparts.
column9: Book tickets 1-7 days in advance online (passport required). If you purchase tickets online with a foreign passport, you must pick up a physical ticket at the entrance to gain entry.
column10: Extremely crowded on weekends and holidays. The cable car is recommended for those with limited mobility. Wear non-slip athletic shoes; many of the steps have a slope of more than 45 degrees. A sun hat is required.

column7: The Summer Palace, a royal garden during the Qing Dynasty in China, is most notable for its perfect combination of natural landscapes surrounded by mountains and water and magnificent palace architecture, skillfully blending natural beauty with human craftsmanship.
column8: Tower of Buddhist Incense, Kunming Lake boat rides, the Long Corridor.
column9: Enter via North Palace Gate for the Suzhou Street start or East Palace Gate for main halls. Boat rides across Kunming Lake are highly recommended.
column10: It is a massive park; wear comfortable walking shoes. Tower of Buddhist Incense requires an extra ticket.

column7: Beijing’s most popular Buddhist temple. It’s famous for its active worship and a breathtaking 18-meter-tall Buddha statue carved from a single piece of sandalwood.
column8: The 18-meter Sandalwood Buddha, Tibetan art, blessing rituals.
column9: Free incense is provided at the entrance. Visit early in the morning to witness monk chanting.
column10: Dress modestly (no short skirts/sleeveless tops). Photography is strictly prohibited inside the Buddha halls. Keep quite inside the Buddhahalls.

column7: The city's top theme park. The Harry Potter world is the biggest draw.You can check out the Transformers live show.
column8: Harry Potter and the Forbidden Journey, Decepticoaster, Butterbeer.
column9: Visitors can download the Beijing Universal Resort app in advance to check queue and show times.Visitors can download the Beijing Universal Resort app in advance to check queue and show times.
column10: Arriving early to avoid peak hours will provide a better experience.

column7: A legendary shopping street blending old-school Beijing heritage with modern malls. It’s a great spot for a night stroll and some souvenir hunting. A legendary shopping street blending old-school Beijing heritage with modern malls. It’s a great spot for a night stroll and some souvenir hunting.
column8: Time-honored brand shops, Wangfujing Bookstore, Heping Guoju (retro underground Beijing).
column9: Visit the Hepingguo (Peace Fruit) underground for a nostalgic "Old Beijing" market experience. Large shopping malls like APM are here.
column10: The famous snack street has been renovated/relocated. Local specialties are more expensive than in other areas. It's crowded on weekends and holidays.
column11: Modern, Foodie, Local Snacks, Takeaway, Citywalk

column7: This is the tomb of 13 Ming Dynasty emperors. At the Dingling Underground Palace, you can descend deep underground and enter the ancient stone-built mausoleum.
column8: Dingling Underground Palace, Ling'en Hall of Changling, Sacred Way stone statues.
column9: Dingling (underground palace) and Changling (largest hall) are the primary sites to visit. Walk the Sacred Way to see the stone statues. Recommended self-driving tour
column10: The area is far from the city center, and the sites are spread out, so renting a car or joining a tour group is more convenient than using public transportation.

column7: A peaceful park used by emperors to worship the Earth God. It’s a local favorite in autumn when the gingko trees turn bright yellow.
column8: Gingko Avenue, the Altar of Earth, authentic local morning exercises.
column9: On a sunny afternoon, the sunlight shining on the ancient altar wall creates a shimmering effect, just like the surface of the sea. The falling leaves of ginkgo trees in autumn are very beautiful.
column10: Very crowded on weekends and holidays. This place carries a profound sense of Chinese literature. It is recommended to visit after understanding the relevant background for a better experience.
column11: Park, Historical, Local Life, Culture, Nature

column7: It located in Tiananmen Square, it is a grand complex. It houses everything from ancient bronze treasures to modern historical artifacts, making it a must-visit for history enthusiasts.
column8: Ancient bronze treasures (Houmuwu Ding), the "Ancient China" exhibit, view of Tiananmen Square.
column9: Free admission but must book online days in advance via official website/WeChat. Passport is required for entry.
column10: Closed on Mondays. No food or liquids allowed inside; bag checks are rigorous. Pay attention to the queue signs and avoid getting lost or joining the wrong queue.

column7: The massive steel stadium from the 2008 Olympics. Most people come here just to snap a photo of its unique exterior, especially at night when it glows red.
column8: Intricate steel structure, the Sky Walk, night-time red lighting.
column9: The Bird's Nest at night is especially spectacular when viewed from the outside after closing time.
column10: Closed on mondays. Internal tours require separate tickets. External light usage time varies with the seasons

column7: Right across from the Bird's Nest, famous for its blue "bubble" walls. It’s now a water park and looks amazing when it lights up in different colors at night.
column8: The "bubble" exterior, indoor water park, colorful night lighting.
column9: Best viewed at night when the light shows are active. The Water Cube is now an "Ice Cube" with a skating rink.
column10: Olympic Park is vast; prepare for lots of walking.

column7: A historic shopping street south of Tiananmen. It has old-fashioned trams and classic shops. It‘s a bit touristy, but great for soaking up old Beijing vibes.
column8: The "Dang Dang" tram, century-old brand shops (Dashilar), historic architecture.
column9: Ride the vintage "Dangdang" tram car. Walk into the side street "Dashilan" . Eat traditional snacks.
column10: Avoid the "tours" offered by strangers on the street.
column11: Historical, Architecture, Local Life, Foodie, Citywalk

column7: A lake district surrounded by hutongs. You can boat in summer, skate in winter, or hit the lakeside bars at night.It's where history meets nightlife.
column8: Yinding Bridge mountain view, Houhai bar street, rickshaw tours.
column9: Visitors can experience the Grain Rain Cultural Festival and watch dragon boat races in the summer. In winter, they can enjoy traditional ice and snow activities, including ice curling, ice ball games, and ice sleds.
column10: Avoid buying dried fruit from unpackaged street vendors.
column11: River View, Historical, Local Life, Citywalk, Nature

column7: It is one of the oldest royal gardens and a great place to rent a boat to explore the beautiful scenery around the Forbidden City, making it a truly relaxing destination.
column8: The White Dagoba, Nine-Dragon Screen, boating on the lake.
column9: Take a ferry to Qionghua Island to see the White Dagoba. Best visited in summer for lotus blossoms.
column10: Closed on Mondays. The climb to the White Dagoba involves many stairs.
column11: Park, Imperial, Historical, Nature, River View

column7: The former home of Heshen, a famous Qing dynasty official. It’s known for the "Fu" (Good Fortune) stele in the garden and is even more ornate than some palaces.
column8: The "Fu" (Good Fortune) Stele, the elaborate back garden, the "Treasury Wall."
column9: Look for the "Fu" (Fortune) stele hidden in the secret garden cave. Audio guides explain the complex feng shui layout.
column10: Closed on Mondays. Daily visitor capacity is strictly limited. Reservations are required during peak season.  Online reservations require visitors to exchange them for paper tickets on-site.

column7: The heart of Beijing. Most people come for the flag-raising ceremony or to see the massive Gate of Heavenly Peace. it’s a must-see landmark.
column8: Flag-raising ceremony, the Gate of Heavenly Peace, Monument to the People's Heroes.
column9: Reservation is mandatory via the official WeChat mini-program. Arrive very early for the sunrise flag-raising ceremony.
column10: Expect multiple layers of security checks; carry your original passport. Lighters and knives are strictly prohibited.

column7: The ruins of a grand royal park destroyed in the 1800s. The stone pillars at Dashuifa are a haunting but beautiful reminder of the past.
column8: Dashuifa ruins, black swans on the lake, the Western Mansion site.
column9: The "Western Mansions" ruins (Dashuifa) is the most famous area. Use the park electric carts to save time.
column10: Very little shade in the ruins area; bring sun protection.

column7: The ancestor of Peking duck. This grand flagship store has hosted world leaders and is the ideal place to enjoy a formal, traditional Peking duck feast.
column8: Traditional "hung-oven" roasting, historic flagship scale, duck slicing ritual.
column9: The Hepingmen branch is the flagship with grand decor and a dedicated duck museum. There are seasonal dishes, such as spring tea drinks, summer iced desserts, and seasonal limited-edition sauces.
column10: Expect a service charge and high prices. Reservations are recommended for peak hours.

column7: Currently the trendiest duck spot. The big draw here is the view. Some tables look directly out at the Forbidden City’s corner towers.
column8: Golden views of the Palace moat, crispy Peking duck, classic Beijing snack platters.
column9: Ask for a window seat to view the Forbidden City moat while dining. Get a queue number early online via apps like Meituan.
column10: Waiting times for a window seat can exceed 3 hours.  Better to make a reservation for peak hours.

column7: If you're craving traditional copper pot mutton hot pot, this is the place to go. Thinly sliced ​​mutton and rich sesame sauce are their signature dishes.
column8: Copper-pot charcoal fire, paper-thin mutton, traditional sesame dip.
column9: Try the traditional copper pot instant-boiled mutton. The sesame dipping sauce is a must-try.
column10: Copper pots are hot; be careful if dining with children.

column7: This restaurant is a favorite among locals in the Muslim Quarter (Niujie). The meat is top-notch, and their signature dishes include Qianlong cabbage and freshly baked sesame buns.
column8: Fresh hand-cut lamb, legendary sesame buns (Shaobing), local Niujie vibes.
column9: Hand-cut meat has a better texture than machine-cut meat. The meat slices are ready to use as soon as they change color for hot pot.
column10: It's best to queue before peak hours.

column7: A famous local eatery near the Drum Tower. Try the "Chaogan" (stewed liver) and pork buns if you want to eat like a real Beijing local for breakfast.
column8: Liver and intestine stew, fried pork buns, Drum Tower backdrop.
column9: Authentic Beijing breakfast may not be to everyone's liking.
column10: Made from liver, it has a slightly fishy smell; those sensitive to this should try it with caution. Noisy in restaurant.

column7: A Michelin 3-star vegetarian restaurant near Lama Temple. It’s very high-end with a misty, zen atmosphere—perfect for a special, fancy meal.
column8: Michelin 3-star vegetarian dining, courtyard Zen atmosphere, artistic presentation.
column9: This vegetarian restaurant uses mushrooms and nuts to mimic the texture of meat.
column10: Very expensive; smart casual dress code recommended. Very expensive; smart casual dress code recommended. A 15% service charge applies.

column7: A high-end Cantonese restaurant known for its incredible Napoleon cake. A great choice if you want a break from local Beijing flavors.
column8: Crispy roast pork, signature Napoleon cake, premium Cantonese dim sum.
column9: Desserts are a safe bet. Hong Kong-style dim sum and the signature braised beef brisket are also highly recommended. Cantonese dim sum is only served between 11:00 and 14:00 PM.
column10: The restaurant charges a 10% service charge, and some dishes (such as the Peking duck at Cuiyuantang) are not very cost-effective.

column7: This is a classic Beijing bakery that blends modern elements. Selling beautifully designed and creatively crafted pastries, it makes a perfect souvenir.
column8: Creative Beijing pastries, cute snack designs, traditional-meets-modern gifts.
column9: This specific shop offers creative, freshly baked pastries in unique shapes like lions and gourds. Great for edible souvenirs. This specific shop offers creative, freshly baked pastries in unique shapes like lions and gourds. Great for edible souvenirs.
column10: There is usually a long queue.
column11: Local Snacks, Takeaway, Must-try, Foodie, Culture

column7: A highly popular local eatery located just east of the Forbidden City. Their signature dish is the Meat Pie. The crust is rolled incredibly thin, while the meat filling inside is thick, juicy, and packed with flavor.
column8: Golden crispy crust, juicy meat filling, home-style comfort food.
column9: It is famous for its "roubing" (meat pie); it is quick to serve and comes in both sweet and savory flavors.
column10: Small restaurant; expect to share tables during lunch rush.

column7: This is the heart of Beijing's Muslim Quarter. If you're looking for the most delicious mutton hot pot, glutinous rice cakes, and tender, juicy beef pies, this is a food paradise.
column8: Authentic Halal food, historic mosque, street snacks.
column9: Bai Ji Rice Cake, Man Ji Baked Cake, Yue Sheng Zhai's Fried Pork, Yi Bao Lotus Leaf Steamed Rice Cake, Cheese Wei are recommended.
column10: Foods made with glutinous rice should be eaten while they are hot, as they will taste worse when they cool down.

column7: A century-old alley near Shichahai. It’s famous for its unique slanted layout and is packed with quirky souvenir shops and boutiques.
column8: Centuries-old "Pipe" alley, post office with custom stamps, lake connection.
column9: Explore the surrounding hutongs by rickshaw. You can get a special postmark at the Qing Dynasty post office counter and send a handwritten postcard. Yandai Street is an ideal place to buy traditional souvenirs.
column10: Bars around the lake can be very loud and overpriced at night. The alleyways are mostly paved with stone slabs, so it is recommended to wear comfortable shoes.
column11: Historical, Citywalk, Local Life, Instagram, Architecture

column7: A trendy yet quiet Hutong. It's filled with designer etudios, independent bookstores and stylish cafes. Much more peaceful than other Hutong.
column8: Designer studios, vintage bookstores, quiet artistic vibes.
column9: Beijing's signature "Erba Coffee," paper-cut maps, and unique souvenirs.
column10: Taking photos at the "Beijing Flavor Time" and "Song Family Calligraphy and Painting" attractions requires making a purchase before it is permitted at these special scenic spots.

column7: A chic, upscale lounge and terrace bar located in the heart of Sanlitun, known for its polished cocktails and sophisticated crowd.
column8: The outdoor terrace overlooking the bustling Sanlitun crossroads.
column9: Order their signature martinis; the bartenders are among the most skilled in the district.
column10: There is often a dress code; avoid flip-flops or overly casual sportswear.

column7: A massive Soviet-style building with a grand spire. It hosts various expos and is home to the famous Moscow Restaurant.
column8: Soviet architecture, soaring spire, the famous "Moscow Restaurant."
column9: Iconic Soviet-style architecture. Check for rotating trade fairs, concerts, or occasional art exhibitions. Don't miss the "Moscow Restaurant" nearby.
column10: The park is free to enter; exhibitions require separate tickets.

column7: China’s top university. Known for its beautiful "Lotus Pond by Moonlight" and classic brick campus buildings.
column8: The Second Gate (Old Gate), Lotus Pond, red-brick western-style campus.
column9: Public tours are only available during weekends and holidays; reservation via WeChat is required. Visit the West Gate (iconic) and the Old Campus.
column10: Very difficult to get a reservation during peak seasons. Academic buildings are restricted.

column7: This is a massive park where you can drive through lion/tiger habitats or take a caged bus for close-up animal encounters.
column8: Self-drive safari, caged bus predator encounters, interactive lemur feeding.
column9: Buying food provided by the zoo in advance allows you to feed the wild animals up close and ride the miniature train to see a wide variety of animals.
column10: Located in Daxing, far from the city center; plan for a full day trip. You cannot bring your own food. The animals are more enthusiastic about interacting with you in the morning. Need reservation.

column3: Beijing Museum of Arts and Crafts
column7: Located near the Olympic park, it houses national treasures like jade carvings and cloisonné—a must for fans of traditional arts.
column8: Imperial "Four Treasures" (Jade, Ivory, Cloisonné, Lacquer).
column9: Located within the Gongmei Mansion on Wangfujing; features "Four Treasures" of Beijing crafts.
column10: Many items are for sale; distinguish between museum pieces and retail goods. Admission is free, but online reservations are required.

column7: Right across from the Forbidden City. A short hike to the top pavilion gives you the absolute best panoramic view of the palace’s golden roofs.
column8: A panoramic view of the Forbidden City from the top of Jingshan Hill.

column10: It is a short but steep climb.

column7: Beijing’s fashion and nightlife hub. It’s home to flagship stores and the city’s most trendy bars and restaurants.
column8: Taikoo Li street fashion, Bar street nightlife

column10: Be wary of "bar scams" in the smaller, unofficial side alleys; stick to established venues.

column7: While Bacardi is a brand, in the Beijing context, it often refers to the "Bacardi-sponsored" bars or legacy lounge spots in the Gongti/Sanlitun area known for Latin vibes and rum-based cocktails.

column7: A niche, contemporary lounge and bar often associated with the electronic music and sleek "cyber" aesthetic found in Beijing’s newer nightlife spots.

column7: Famous for its massive white stupa. The best way to enjoy it is from a nearby rooftop cafe with a coffee in hand.

column7: A popular mall known for its massive basement "Night Food Street," which stays open late and offers endless dining options.

column7: A legendary market for silk, custom suits, and souvenirs. It’s a favorite for international tourists looking for high-quality bargains.

column7: The site of Peking University was originally the royal garden (Yan Garden) of the Ming and Qing dynasties. The campus has towering ancient trees, Chinese-style pavilions and Western-style red brick buildings.

column7: Beijing’s most famous and longest-running LGBTQ+ club and cultural center. It features multiple floors ranging from a high-energy dance floor to art gallery spaces.

column9: Check their official WeChat for specific "Theme Nights" which can vary from drag shows to art talks.

column7: A sleek, contemporary art museum near Sanlitun. It’s a quiet escape for those looking to see unique exhibitions away from the crowds.

column7: It is a well-known Buddhist temple in central Shanghai. It blends tradition with a modern setting. It is the oldest Buddhist temple in Shanghai, and its architectural style is modeled after structures from before the Ming Dynasty, with the bracket system serving as a prime example.

column9: Less crowded than the Bund. Best in evening.

column7: Lu Xun Park is a public park dedicated to the famous writer. It reflects local daily life.
column8: Popular for walking and relaxation.
column9: A popular local park where you can experience daily life in Shanghai, especially in the morning. You may see people practicing tai chi, singing, or dancing. It’s a good place for a short, relaxing visit.

column7: Great Tang All Day Mall is a large Tang-style pedestrian street inspired by the prosperous Tang Dynasty. At night, its bright lights, performances, and cultural activities recreate the romance and vitality of ancient Chang’an.
column8: Asia’s largest matrix fountain, live shows, historical NPCs, live tumbler performance, Hanfu rental
column9: Performances are usually held between 19:00 and 23:00. The area is large, so wear comfortable shoes. It is useful to bring some cash.
column10: Performance times may change, so check the official schedule in advance. It gets very crowded on public holidays, and driving is not recommended.

column7: The Terracotta Army is part of the Mausoleum of Emperor Qin Shi Huang and is known as one of the "Eight Wonders of the World." Its lifelike clay soldiers, horses, and chariots show the power of the Qin Dynasty and its superb craftsmanship.
column8: Terracotta Warriors, bronze chariots, Eight Wonders of the World, ancient military history, sculpture art
column9: Book tickets 8 days in advance via WeChat and enter with valid ID. The ticket includes both the Terracotta Warriors Museum and Lishan Garden; shuttle buses to Lishan Garden are free. The site is large, so wear comfortable shoes. Guided tours are available inside.
column10: Closed on Mondays. Tickets are not sold on site, and queues may be needed when entering during busy periods.

column7: Mount Hua is one of China’s Five Great Mountains and is famous for its steep and dramatic cliffs. It has five main peaks, beautiful natural scenery, and important Taoist cultural sites.
column8: Plank Walk, mountaintop sunrise, Taoist temples, steep peaks
column9: Book tickets up to 10 days in advance through the WeChat mini program "Huashan Tourism Service Platform," or buy them on site. Mountain lodging ranges is available and should be booked early in peak season if you plan to stay overnight for sunrise.
column10: Hiking requires good physical strength. Prepare sports shoes, trekking poles, enough food and water, sun-protective clothing, a power bank, and other essentials.

column7: Shaanxi History Museum is an important cultural landmark in Xi’an. Its collections, including bronze objects, Tang tri-colored pottery, agate cups, and silver sachets, tell the story of ancient Shaanxi.
column8: Ancient Shaanxi relics, national treasures, rich collections
column9: Reserve free tickets 5 days in advance through the "Shaanxi History Museum" WeChat account. Volunteer guided tours are available at 9:00 and 13:30, and audio guides can also be rented. Special paid exhibitions is also available.
column10: Closed every Monday. Tickets are hard to get, and the museum can be very crowded during peak hours.

column7: Xi’an City Wall is one of the largest and best-preserved ancient city walls in China, with a history of over 600 years. It is about 14 kilometers long and includes four main gates: Changle Gate, Yongning Gate, Anding Gate, and Anyuan Gate.
column8: Ancient Xi’an architecture, city wall sunset, ancient military wisdom
column9: Book tickets up to 6 days in advance via the "Xi’an City Wall Scenic Area" WeChat mini program. Tickets allow multiple entries on the same day. Bicycles can be rented on the wall. In winter, the Spring Festival Lantern Fair requires a separate ticket.
column10: There is little shade on the city wall, so avoid visiting at noon.

column7: Huaqing Pool is an ancient royal garden with a long history. It is famous for its imperial hot springs and is also linked to the romantic story of Emperor Xuanzong and Yang Guifei in the Tang Dynasty.
column8: Royal garden, imperial bathing pools, park performances, architectural art
column9: Tickets can be bought on site or booked in advance through the "Huaqing Palace Scenic Area" WeChat account. The site also hosts two large live-action shows, The Song of Everlasting Sorrow and Xi’an Incident. Guided tours are available in the park.
column10: The scenic area has a complex layout, so allow enough time if you want to explore it fully. The live performances are held at night, so arriving in the afternoon is recommended.

column3: The bell tower The drum tower
column7: The Bell Tower and Drum Tower were important timekeeping landmarks in ancient Xi’an. In the past, the bell was struck in the morning and the drum was beaten in the evening to mark the time.
column8: Chime-bell performance, ancient timekeeping, painted rooftops, ancient architecture
column9: A combined ticket for the Bell Tower and Drum Tower is available. The towers display historical bells and related artifacts. You can also skip entering and take photos from outside, especially at night when the lights make the view more impressive.
column10: The stairs inside the Bell Tower are quite steep. Wear comfortable shoes and lightweight clothing.

column7: Tang Paradise is a large cultural theme park that presents the style and splendor of the Tang Dynasty. Its scenic areas and themed performances showcase the culture and art of this golden age.
column8: Live cultural shows, boat viewing, Tang-style royal garden, Ladies’ Hall, Rosy Cloud Corridor, Giant Wild Goose Pagoda viewpoint
column9: Book tickets up to 7 days in advance through the "Tang Paradise" WeChat account. Tickets allow one-time entry only. Some performances require separate tickets, and the night light show is worth seeing.
column10: The park is large, so wear light and comfortable clothing. In summer, it can be hot and sunny, so protect yourself from heat and sun exposure.

column7: Xi’an Museum consists of the museum, the Tang Jianfu Temple site, and the Small Wild Goose Pagoda. Centered on the theme of ancient Xi’an, it displays representative cultural relics unearthed from different historical periods.
column8: Ancient Xi’an architecture, Tang tri-colored glazed pottery, Sui gilded Buddha statue, AR experience
column9: Reserve free tickets in advance through the Xi’an Museum website or WeChat account; the ticket also includes the Small Wild Goose Pagoda. Audio guides are available inside, and bringing wired earphones can be useful. After visiting the museum, you can also take photos around the outdoor area.
column10: Closed every Tuesday and on Chinese New Year’s Eve. During peak travel seasons, it can be crowded with long queues.

column7: The Giant Wild Goose Pagoda is a Buddhist pagoda inside Da Ci’en Temple. Built by Xuanzang to preserve scriptures and Buddhist images from India, it is one of Xi’an’s major cultural landmarks.
column8: Buddhist site, poetry street, red temple walls, musical fountain
column9: The pagoda is inside Da Ci’en Temple. Climbing the Giant Wild Goose Pagoda requires a separate ticket. You can reserve in advance via the "Da Ci’en Temple" WeChat mini program, and light-colored clothes are recommended for photos.
column10: The musical fountain runs four times a day: 12:00, 16:00, 19:00, and 21:00. Plan your visit accordingly if you want to see the show.

column7: The Small Wild Goose Pagoda is a Buddhist pagoda inside Jianfu Temple. Built to house scriptures translated by the monk Yijing, it is known for its graceful and peaceful appearance.
column8: Ancient Xi’an architecture, Buddhist pagoda, Jin Dynasty iron bell, earthquake-resistant tower
column9: Reserve free tickets in advance through the Xi’an Museum website or WeChat account. The Small Wild Goose Pagoda is inside Xi’an Museum, so one reservation covers both sites.
column10: Closed every Tuesday and on Chinese New Year’s Eve. During peak travel seasons, it can be crowded with long queues.

column7: Daming Palace was the most magnificent of the three main palace complexes of Tang Chang’an. It served as the political center of the Tang Empire, where emperors of the Li-Tang dynasty handled state affairs.
column8: Royal garden, miniature landscapes, archaeology exploration center, VR experience
column9: Tickets to the architectural ruins can be booked through the "Daming Palace National Heritage Park" WeChat account. A combo ticket includes admission, guided explanation, and an electric cart inside the park.
column10: Closed every Tuesday. The park is very large, so wear comfortable clothing; in summer, protect yourself from heat and sun exposure.

column7: Gao Family Courtyard is one of the largest and best-preserved official residence compounds in Xi’an’s old city. It offers a glimpse into traditional architecture and local family life.
column8: Traditional courtyard residence, shadow puppetry, Huayin folk opera
column9: Tickets can be bought on site. On rainy days, you may see the traditional "four waters return to the hall" courtyard drainage scene. Shadow puppet shows and northern Shaanxi folk performances are usually available on regular days.
column10: Closed on Wednesdays for restoration. Performance seats are limited.
column11: Historical, Architecture, Culture, Local Life, Art

column7: Xi’an Beilin Museum is an art museum focused on ancient stone tablets, epitaphs, and stone carvings. It is known as "China’s largest stone library."
column8: Ancient calligraphy, stone carving art
column9: Reserve tickets 5 days in advance through the "Xi’an Beilin Museum" WeChat account. To avoid crowds, enter from the north gate.
column10: Wear comfortable non-slip flat shoes. Food is not allowed inside the museum.

column7: Ancient Guanyin Temple is a thousand-year-old Buddhist temple in the Zhongnan Mountains. It is famous for a 1,400-year-old ginkgo tree said to have been planted by Emperor Taizong of the Tang Dynasty.
column8: Ancient famous tree, Guanyin sacred spring, legendary stories
column9: Reserve 3 days in advance through the "Zhongnan Chanyun" WeChat account. The back hill offers beautiful overhead views of the ginkgo tree and the Zhongnan Mountains. Ginkgo sachets are available as souvenirs inside the temple.
column10: Dress respectfully for the temple setting. Do not photograph monks during your visit.

You can break the flatbread by hand or use a machine; the smaller the pieces, the better they absorb the soup. The Great Tang All Day Mall branch also offers views of the Giant Wild Goose Pagoda.

column9: Book tickets 10 days in advance through the "Huaqing Palace Scenic Area" WeChat account. Area A offers the most immersive experience, Area B has AR glasses rental, Area C has good views and better value, while Areas D/E may have partial view obstruction.

column7: Muslim Quarter was once the center of Xi’an’s Hui Muslim community. Today, it is a lively food and cultural street filled with a wide variety of classic Xi’an snacks.

column7: Tang West Market is a cultural tourism complex built on the site of the Tang Dynasty’s West Market. It features shopping for antiques, jade, jewelry, calligraphy and paintings, tea, and tea ware.

column9: Tang West Market includes both a museum and a shopping center.
column10: Be cautious when buying antiques and jade. In summer, protect yourself from the heat.

column7: Joy City is an international shopping mall that blends Tang Dynasty culture with modern trends. It offers shopping, dining, and entertainment in a stylish commercial space.
column8: Sky terrace, rooftop garden, shopping mall
column9: This is a pet-friendly mall, and pets are allowed inside.

column7: 21Club Xi'an is an American retro-style speakeasy bar inspired by the Prohibition era. It is known for its hidden entrance, lively social atmosphere, and vintage interior design, making it popular with outgoing visitors and local nightlife lovers.

column10: Smoking may be allowed indoors, and it can get crowded on weekends and public holidays.

column7: The iconic heart of Chongqing, originally built to commemorate the victory in the War of Resistance. Today it’s a vibrant hub for shopping, dining, and nightlife, and the go-to starting point for most visitors.
column8: Lively at night. Pedestrian street packed with street snacks.
column9: Best to visit Monument itself during the day. The surrounding pedestrian streets are more vibrant at night.
column10: Can get quite crowded.

column7: A typical hillside residential complex known for its layered, maze-like structure and direct street access without elevators, making it a perfect example of Chongqing’s "8D city" layout.
column8: Popular photo spot. Three exits on the 1st, 10th, and 15th floors. Fully connected corridors throughout the building.
column9: Follow the signs to get around. Photo spot: Cable cars pass roughly every 10 minutes. Fewer crowds before 10 AM.
column10: Residential area. Please keep quiet. No elevators. Can be confusing to navigate due to its multi-level layout.

column7: Hongyadong, sits at the confluence of the Yangtze and Jialing Rivers. Built along the hillside, its 13-level complex of traditional stilted buildings is a designated 4A scenic area, famed for its dramatic night illumination, and is now one of the city’s most iconic landmarks.
column8: Stunning light displays at night. Interior mainly focused on dining and entertainment. Cyberpunk.
column9: Main entrances are on Levels 11, 2, and 1. Lights are typically on from 19:30 to 23:00.
column10: Visit the interior during the day, as it gets extremely crowded at night—better for viewing from the outside then.

column7: A major bridge spanning the Jialing River, and one of the best spots to capture Hongyadong at night. Offers wide, open views with stunning city lights.
column8: Nighttime lighting. Classic photo spot for capturing Hongyadong.
column9: Lights on from 19:00 to 21:30. Can walk up the bridge from the Hongyadong side and head down to the riverside on the other end.
column10: Very heavy traffic and crowds after lighting at night. Taxis may not reach the bridge—best to walk up.

column7: Chongqing City Zoo is famous for its pandas. The park is fairly large, making it ideal for a relaxed half-day visit and especially popular with families.
column8: A wide range of rare and protected animals, including pandas and South China tigers.
column9: Tickets can be purchased on-site or online via WeChat mini programs. Opening hours vary by enclosure, check the official website (http://www.cqzoo.com) in advance to plan your visit. Best to go in the morning when animals are more active, as they tend to rest in the heat or at noon.
column10: Last entry is at 17:00

column7: A former industrial site transformed into a creative park, featuring art spaces, cafés, and design shops. A popular spot for photos and hangouts among young visitors.
column8: A popular citywalk route. Retro-art vibe.
column9: Best entered from the south gate of Eling Park, visit the park first and then walk straight into the Erchang. Most shops open between 9:00 - 22:00.
column10: Most of the shops are quite commercialized. May not be worth a visit if visitors don't like taking photos.

column7: Famous for trains passing directly through a residential building, this viral spot showcases Chongqing’s unique multi-level urban transport.
column8: Unique architecture. Iconic photo spot.
column9: Exit from Liziba Station Exit 2 and follow the signs down to street level, the classic photo spot is across the road.
column10: Crowded during holidays. Trains run about every 5 minutes, so expect some waiting to get the perfect shot.

column7: A grand public building in traditional Chinese architectural style, built under the direction of early Chinese revolutionary leaders. Located opposite the Three Gorges Museum, it stands as one of Chongqing’s key cultural landmarks.
column8: Traditional Chinese architecture.
column9: Tickets can be purchased on-site via QR code for 8 CNY. The exterior is the main highlight—while the interior is open for photos, but not much to see.
column10: Ticket sales stop at 17:30. Closed to visitors during performances or major events.

column7: One of Chongqing’s busiest commercial districts, combining shopping malls, restaurants, and bars, and a top choice for visitors to eat and explore.
column8: Multiple malls and popular photo spots. Wide variety of food options.
column9: Start from Guanyinqiao Metro Station (Exits 4 or 7). For local food, head to "观音桥好吃街" The pedestrian bridge near its north gate features the famous "我在重庆 (I am in Chongqing)" LED screen. For anime and pop culture, check out "方圆 live." The area is especially lively at night.
column10: Heavy crowds due to many popular photo spots so visitors should plan route based on interests. Best to go earlier in the day during summer

column7: A hillside walking route linking old streets and residential areas, offering a close-up look at Chongqing’s unique terrain and local life.
column8: Experience Chongqing’s unique terrain and local life. Scenic river views along the way.
column9: Setting "佳实养老院" as the starting point allows for a mostly downhill route. Along the way, small creative shops offer local souvenirs and street snacks. Often combined with a visit to Shibati, first walking trail, then Shibati.
column10: Comfortable sneakers are recommended. Expect steep stairs and narrow paths; extra caution is advised during busy periods. Take precautions against the heat in summer.
column11: Hiking, Citywalk, Local Life, Local Snacks, Photography, Historical

column7: A historic district connecting Chongqing’s upper and lower city, preserving the character of old Chongqing while now blending culture with modern commerce.
column8: Old Chongqing city atmosphere. Night views. Tunnel relics.
column9: Often combined with a visit to Shibati, first walking trail, then Shibati. The atmosphere is best after dark. When visiting directly, starting from Jiaochangkou Exit 11 allows for a mostly downhill route.
column10: Comfortable sneakers are recommended. Avoid buying souvenirs inside the area.
column11: Historical, Culture, Citywalk, Local Life, Photography, Local Snacks

column7: A classic ferry experience offering close-up views of the Yangtze and Jialing rivers, especially at their confluence.
column8: Ferry ride. Chongqing river views.
column9: Tickets available on-site or via WeChat mini programs, around 15 CNY per person. One-way ride takes about 15 minutes. Departures also available from Danzishi Dock (弹子石码头). Multiple routes to choose from.
column10: Arriving early helps secure better views on board. Service frequency is limited, so checking departure times in advance via the mini program is recommended.

column7: A thousand-year-old town and a microcosm of Chongqing, playing a key role in the city’s cultural heritage and a popular place to experience traditional local life.
column8: Stroll through the old town and soak in the slower pace of old Chongqing.
column9: Taking a taxi directly to the main entrance（磁器口入口） is recommended. Enter from the front, explore at a relaxed pace, and exit via the back streets. Stopping at an open-air teahouse for a break is a great option.
column10: Dining and souvenir shopping here are generally not recommended.
column11: Historical, Culture, Local Snacks, Citywalk, Must-see

column7: An observation deck at the top of Raffles City, featuring high-altitude attractions such as the Skywalk and Cloud Swing, with panoramic views over Chongqing’s two rivers.
column8: A combination of skyline views and thrilling high-altitude experiences.
column9: Booking tickets at least one day in advance via Dianping is recommended. Sunset is the best time for attractions such as the Cloud Swing. Skirts, slippers, and loose accessories are not recommended.
column10: Not suitable for those with a fear of heights. All attractions have age and height restrictions—check requirements before booking. Last entry at 21:15

column7: A viral photo spot known for its dramatic angle and safety harness setup, creating a cinematic cyberpunk-style "high-rise agent" look against Chongqing’s skyline.
column8: Cyberpunk atmosphere. City night views.
column9: Photo packages with photography services should be booked at least one day in advance via Dianping, though hiring a personal photographer is also possible. Queue numbers are collected on-site on the day of entry. Dark-colored outfits are recommended for a stronger cyberpunk aesthetic.
column10: After attaching the safety harness, the shooting position is roughly two stories above the platform. Not recommended for those with a strong fear of heights. Guidance is provided before shooting, with around 3–5 minutes allocated per person; delays caused by personal issues such as fear of heights are generally not accommodated.

column7: A large theme park ideal for entertainment and family visits, especially suitable for those traveling with children.
column8: A wide range of thrilling rides and live performances.
column9: Tickets can be purchased on-site or online. Admission covers both day and night sessions, with the night session running from 17:00 to 22:00.
column10: Food inside the park is relatively expensive—bringing snacks and water is recommended. Long queues are common during holidays. Prices may vary during peak seasons or special events.

column7: An aerial cable car crossing the Yangtze River, offering panoramic views of the city and river, and one of Chongqing’s most iconic experiences.
column8: Crossing the Yangtze River. One of the best ways to enjoy Chongqing’s night skyline.
column9: Nearby attractions include Baixiangju, Jiefangbei, Shibati, and Hongyadong on the north side, and Xiahaoli and Longmenhao Old Street on the south side. Tickets are best booked in advance via the "长江索道" official WeChat mini program. Visiting after dark offers the most impressive city views.
column10: Tickets are time-slot based with queue numbers (e.g., 17:00–17:59). Arrival at the start of the time window is recommended, with total waiting time around one hour—plan accordingly. Opening hours may change in different periods.
column11: Must-see, River View, Photography, Immersive, Landmark, Instagram

column7: One of the largest single shopping complexes in China, made up of five sections (A–E) with over 1,200 brands, focusing on accessible, non-luxury retail and popular among young locals.
column8: A comprehensive experience covering dining, shopping, and entertainment.
column9: All sections are connected by walkways. In addition to shopping, a wide range of services can be found, including restaurants, bars, and salons. Fully air-conditioned in summer, making it a good place to escape the heat.
column10: The complex is really large, so comfortable shoes are recommended, and sufficient time should be planned for a full visit.

column7: An emerging artsy district blending historic architecture with modern design, ideal for a slow stroll and photography.
column8: A relaxed glimpse into Chongqing’s local lifestyle.
column9: Best visited around sunset, with the most beautiful views after dark. The most popular photo spots are the Waterfall Viewing Platform and Haoyue Bookstore Terrace, with clear signage throughout the area. Most shops operate between 10:00 and 22:00.
column10: The area is mostly uphill, so comfortable, non-slip shoes are recommended. Viewing platforms can get crowded, so allow extra time. Take sun protection and stay cool in summer. Steps may be slippery on rainy days.

column7: A shopping mall that gained popularity for its environment and design, known as a social media hotspot.
column8: The "Luminous Forest" indoor garden, featuring lush greenery reminiscent of Singapore-style design.
column9: Direct access from Chongguang Road Station (Metro Line 5, Exit 1). Plenty of dining options nearby, though shopping variety is less comprehensive compared to places like MixC Mall. Most visually appealing on sunny summer days, with full air conditioning available.
column10: Best experienced on sunny days when natural light shines into the space; less impressive in other weather conditions.

column7: A long-running local favorite featured on Chongqing’s must-eat lists, serving a wide variety of traditional Chongqing home-style dishes for over 20 years.
column8: Retro local-style restaurant. Offers a broad range of flavors—from light and savory to sweet, sour, and spicy—providing a great introduction to local Chongqing cuisine.
column9: The menu is handwritten in Chinese with no photos—ordering via Dianping based on pictures of recommended dishes is helpful. Top picks include Steamed Pork Ribs with Millet, Signature Soy Sauce Chicken, and Fish-Fragrant Shredded Pork. Suitable for those who prefer mild flavors but still want to experience local food.
column10: Queues are common during peak hours—taking a number in advance via Dianping is recommended. Tissues are charged separately. Closed during the Chinese New Year period.

column7: A classic neighborhood hot pot restaurant with over 30 years of history, known for its authentic Chongqing flavors.
column8: Authentic Chongqing hot pot. Fresh ingredients and a clean dining environment.
column9: For those sensitive to spice, a 鸳鸯 (yuan-yang) pot (half spicy, half mild broth) is recommended. Spice levels are adjustable, mild spicy is suitable for most. The menu is in Chinese only, so ordering based on Dianping recommendations is helpful. Classic local ingredients such as beef tripe, duck blood, and braised chicken feet are worth trying depending on preference. For a break from the heat, try the brown sugar iced tofu pudding or chilled sweet rice balls available in-store.
column10: Queues are common during peak hours and holidays. Reservations are only available by phone or in person—visiting off-peak or allowing extra waiting time is recommended. Hot pot can be messy and has a strong smell, clothes that already worn or clothes that can handle stains are recommended. Open during the Chinese New Year, though crowds may be heavier.

column7: A well-loved local restaurant with over 10 years of history, known for its signature Chongqing-style grilled fish and popular for group dining.
column8: Live fish prepared fresh to order. Bold, spicy flavors that pair well with rice.
column9: A variety of fish options are available, Jiangtuan (longsnout catfish) is highly recommended as it has very few bones. Signature flavors include garlic and spicy; note that even the garlic option has some heat. Locals often pair the meal with desserts like liangxia or shaved ice from nearby shops. The menu is in Chinese only, so ordering via Dianping recommendations is helpful.
column10: Outdoor seating and accessible facilities are available. Queue numbers can be taken in advance via Dianping. Open during the Chinese New Year.

column7: A classic Chongqing jianghu cuisine restaurant, best known for its signature "huobao" (intense stir-fry) dishes.
column8: Traditional jianghu-style cooking. Bold, spicy flavors that pair well with rice and drinks.
column9: The signature "手撕鸭" (shredded duck) is savory and not spicy. The "火爆" (huobao) series features rich, aromatic, and spicy flavors—popular choices include huobao beef tripe and huobao pork intestines, perfect with drinks.
column10: Not ideal for those who cannot handle any spice. Reservations are by phone only. Lunch is usually less busy, while dinner can get crowded (around 30 minutes for waiting). Closed during the Chinese New Year period.

column7: A local noodle shop with a casual, everyday vibe, offering authentic flavors and a glimpse into Chongqing’s daily food culture.
column8: Affordable prices. Classic noodle dishes.
column9: Ordering can be done by scanning a QR code at the table. Mild spice is recommended for most. Signature items include pea & minced pork noodles, Chongqing noodles (plain), and red chili wontons. Noodle portions come in two sizes: "二两 er liang" (small) and "三两 san liang" (large).
column10: Open all day. Complimentary soup and side dishes are available.

column7: A local favorite dessert shop known for its handmade traditional sweet soups, made from ingredients like rice, beans, and milk.
column8: Light, low-sugar desserts at affordable prices. Refreshing and made to order.
column9: Focused on low-sugar options, making it a good choice for those looking for something refreshing without too much sweetness. The menu includes photos, and ordering can be done by scanning a QR code at the table.
column10: Multiple locations across Chongqing—opening hours may vary by branch.

column7: A hillside barbecue restaurant overlooking Jiefangbei and the Yuzhong skyline, combining stunning night views with live music and a lively atmosphere.
column8: A perfect mix of Chongqing night views and barbecue dining.
column9: Traffic on the hillside can be heavy—walking about 10 minutes from Shangxinjie Station is recommended. Arriving before sunset on a clear day offers the best transition from daylight views to nighttime scenery with live music. Vegetable dishes can be refilled for free.
column10: Reservations are not available, so arriving early for a good seat is recommended.

column7: A riverside fusion barbecue restaurant popular for its night views and unique indoor wave projection design.
column8: A combination of barbecue dining and scenic views. Live music performances.
column9: Seats facing the river are the most recommended. Visiting around sunset on a clear day offers the best experience, while the indoor wave projection becomes more visible after dark.
column10: Reservations can be made in advance via Dianping. Without a reservation, waiting times are usually around 30 minutes.
column11: Foodie, River View, Cuisine, Instagram, Photography

column7: One of Chongqing’s most popular rooftop river-view gardens, offering 360-degree views of the skyline, paired with cocktails, dining, and an energetic atmosphere.
column8: Wide cocktail selection. Front-row river views. DJ-supported nightlife atmosphere.
column9: Live band performances run daily from 19:00 to 22:00, with no minimum spending requirement. River-facing seats are highly recommended. Drone shows can sometimes be seen on weekend nights.
column10: Reservations are recommended for river-view seating.
column11: Skyline, River View, Modern, Instagram, Photography, Bar

column7: A highly rated specialty bar in Chongqing, known for its stylish atmosphere and relaxed setting, ideal for small gatherings with friends.
column8: Creative cocktail menu. Comfortable and elegant environment.
column9: Originally founded in Yunnan, the bar offers several regionally inspired cocktails with flavors rarely found elsewhere. Staff provide detailed introductions and recommended ways to enjoy each drink.
column10: Private rooms require a minimum spend.
